EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds gusted between roughly 30 and 50 mph across parts of southwest Virginia during the late morning and afternoon hours. While these winds were not in the typical range that would prompt trees to be blown down, recent rains on April 20th helped yield a moist ground that allowed trees to come down more easily. In Lynchburg, a fallen tree landed on a parked car. In Carroll County, a large oak tree came down on Riverhill Road near Riverhill Church. The greatest abundance of trees fell in Franklin County. Trees came down in or near the communities of Hardy, Rocky Mount, Ferrum, and Henry. Some of these trees blocked roads, and one tree, as it fell, brought down a power line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew a tree down on a car on Main Street in Lynchburg between 11th Street and 12th Street. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southerly flow resulted in very warm moist air entering the region leading up to the early morning hours of March 5th. Precipitable water values ranged from one inch west of interstate 77 to around 1.25 inches east of the Blue Ridge. During the evening of March 4th, a Miller A low pressure system passed across the southeast states, radiating moisture northward along the Appalachian chain, with areas of heavy rain observed across southeast West Virginia into the Mountain Empire and Alleghany Highland regions of Virginia. Arctic air built in from the west during the early morning hours of March 5th, causing rain to change over to areas of freezing rain, sleet and snow. Highest elevations along the interstate 64 changed over to a wintry mix before dawn, with the changeover gradually working into the lower elevations across the mountains around sunrise, and then east of the Blue Ridge during late morning and early afternoon. A wave of low pressure passed across the Piedmont during the late morning of the 5th, enhancing wintry precipitation across the region. As the wave passed north during the afternoon, precipitation came to an end from west to east as winds shifted northwesterly, allowing drier arctic air to move into the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inches of snow fell across portions of Craig County, Virginia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heavy rainfall event with slow-moving boundary produced significant small stream and subsequent river flooding in the mountains of southwest VA beginning overnight Sunday, April 19th. Rainfall amounts totaled 1 to 3 inches through 7:00AM EDT Monday, April 20th with isolated amounts up to 4 inches (4.00 measured at Galax, VA Water Plant COOP). Severe weather broke out later in the day on Monday. Heavy rain (2-2 �� inches) fell in one hour in Wythe County, VA Monday evening causing flash flooding and road washouts. One of the more impressive stream responses occurred on Reed Creek in Wythe County where the gage at Grahams Forge (GRHV2) crested at 9.14 feet (discharge of 11600 cfs) on the afternoon of the 20th (Flood Stage = 7 feet). This was the 2nd highest stage since continuous records began there in the late 1920s and in terms of flood frequency equates to roughly a 33-year event (Annual Exceedance Probability, AEP of 0.03) per the latest USGS Flood Frequency Report. The highest stage recorded at this gage occurred in April 1977 at 10.01 feet during a period of major flooding in southwest Virginia. Local emergency management official said the flooding was ���historic��� in that area. Over 50 roads were reported to have been closed in Wythe County by flood waters during the event with substantial road damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains led to the closure of numerous roads across Wythe County. Among the roads closed were Glade Road, Reed Creek Drive, Kents Lane, Ramsey Mountain Road, Francis Mill Road, Cedar Springs Road, Dry Road, Ridge Avenue, Atkins Mill Road at Muskrat Road, Brea Road, Church and Baumgardner Roads. Stony Fork Road was also closed due to massive amounts of water in the area. A pipe under the road at the intersection of Stony Fork Road and Griffith Knob Lane collapsed, causing the road to wash away and leaving a 40-foot sinkhole in this area. There was also flood damage at the town of Wytheville Water Treatment Plant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southerly flow resulted in very warm moist air entering the region leading up to the early morning hours of March 5th. Precipitable water values ranged from one inch west of interstate 77 to around 1.25 inches east of the Blue Ridge. During the evening of March 4th, a Miller A low pressure system passed across the southeast states, radiating moisture northward along the Appalachian chain, with areas of heavy rain observed across southeast West Virginia into the Mountain Empire and Alleghany Highland regions of Virginia. Arctic air built in from the west during the early morning hours of March 5th, causing rain to change over to areas of freezing rain, sleet and snow. Highest elevations along the interstate 64 changed over to a wintry mix before dawn, with the changeover gradually working into the lower elevations across the mountains around sunrise, and then east of the Blue Ridge during late morning and early afternoon. A wave of low pressure passed across the Piedmont during the late morning of the 5th, enhancing wintry precipitation across the region. As the wave passed north during the afternoon, precipitation came to an end from west to east as winds shifted northwesterly, allowing drier arctic air to move into the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 7.8 inches in several locations of Rockbridge County, while a few locations received lesser amounts of 4.8 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds gusted between roughly 30 and 50 mph across parts of southwest Virginia during the late morning and afternoon hours. While these winds were not in the typical range that would prompt trees to be blown down, recent rains on April 20th helped yield a moist ground that allowed trees to come down more easily. In Lynchburg, a fallen tree landed on a parked car. In Carroll County, a large oak tree came down on Riverhill Road near Riverhill Church. The greatest abundance of trees fell in Franklin County. Trees came down in or near the communities of Hardy, Rocky Mount, Ferrum, and Henry. Some of these trees blocked roads, and one tree, as it fell, brought down a power line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds snapped off an oak tree that was 36 inches in circumference onto Riverhill Road, one-quarter mile away from Riverhill Church.

Trees came down in or near the communities of Hardy, Rocky Mount, Ferrum, and Henry. Some of these trees blocked roads, and one tree, as it fell, brought down a power line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds brought many trees down across Franklin County. Near the community of Hardy, a tree was downed and blocked the roadway near the intersection of Setzer Way and Hardy Road. Another downed tree blocked the roadway near the intersection of Edwardsville Road and Fieldstone Lane. Near or in the community of Rocky Mount a tree blocked Iron Ridge Road in the 9300 block, near Grassy Hill Road. A fallen tree blocked one lane of Pell Avenue, and another tree blocked the entire roadway of Cliff Street. In addition, a tree blocked the Angle Plantation road in the 500 block. A power line was brought down by a fallen tree in the 2500 block of Rakes Road. Near or in the community of Ferrum, a tree blocked the roadway in the 600 block of Ferrum School Road. Additionally, another tree blocked the roadway in the 1000 block of Saint John's Loop. In the community of Henry, a tree was downed near the intersection of Henry Road and Providence Church Road. Damage values are estimated.

Rainfall amounts totaled 1 to 3 inches through 7:00AM EDT Monday, April 20th with isolated amounts up to 4 inches (4.00 measured at Galax, VA Water Plant COOP). Severe weather broke out later in the day on Monday. Heavy rain (2-2 �� inches) fell in one hour in Wythe County, VA Monday evening causing flash flooding and road washouts. One of the more impressive stream responses occurred on Reed Creek in Wythe County where the gage at Grahams Forge (GRHV2) crested at 9.14 feet (discharge of 11600 cfs) on the afternoon of the 20th (Flood Stage = 7 feet). This was the 2nd highest stage since continuous records began there in the late 1920s and in terms of flood frequency equates to roughly a 33-year event (Annual Exceedance Probability, AEP of 0.03) per the latest USGS Flood Frequency Report. The highest stage recorded at this gage occurred in April 1977 at 10.01 feet during a period of major flooding in southwest Virginia. Local emergency management official said the flooding was ���historic��� in that area. Over 50 roads were reported to have been closed in Wythe County by flood waters during the event with substantial road damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water of 1-2 foot depth was reported flowing across Hagers Spring Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple cellular convection formed during the early afternoon across eastern Kentucky and southern West Virginia, then moved into far western Virginia late in the afternoon.  A few storms pulsed to stronger levels.  Brief downpours also caused full ditch lines along roads and muddy swollen creeks.  A gauge along Slate Creek measured 0.99 inches in just 15 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell along Route 640 and Hale Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low formed over Texas, then quickly moved east during the day and overnight, pushing off the Carolina coast by the morning of the 17th. A very cold airmass in place from retreating Arctic high pressure resulted in higher than average snow ratios, between 12:1 and 15:1. Central Virginia received the highest amounts, with lower amounts to the north and west.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 4.0 and 6.0 inches was reported by multiple sources in the county and surrounding areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southerly flow resulted in very warm moist air entering the region leading up to the early morning hours of March 5th. Precipitable water values ranged from one inch west of interstate 77 to around 1.25 inches east of the Blue Ridge. During the evening of March 4th, a Miller A low pressure system passed across the southeast states, radiating moisture northward along the Appalachian chain, with areas of heavy rain observed across southeast West Virginia into the Mountain Empire and Alleghany Highland regions of Virginia. Arctic air built in from the west during the early morning hours of March 5th, causing rain to change over to areas of freezing rain, sleet and snow. Highest elevations along the interstate 64 changed over to a wintry mix before dawn, with the changeover gradually working into the lower elevations across the mountains around sunrise, and then east of the Blue Ridge during late morning and early afternoon. A wave of low pressure passed across the Piedmont during the late morning of the 5th, enhancing wintry precipitation across the region. As the wave passed north during the afternoon, precipitation came to an end from west to east as winds shifted northwesterly, allowing drier arctic air to move into the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 7.5 inches across Bath County, Virginia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heavy rainfall event with slow-moving boundary produced significant small stream and subsequent river flooding in the mountains of southwest VA beginning overnight Sunday, April 19th. Rainfall amounts totaled 1 to 3 inches through 7:00AM EDT Monday, April 20th with isolated amounts up to 4 inches (4.00 measured at Galax, VA Water Plant COOP). Severe weather broke out later in the day on Monday. Heavy rain (2-2 �� inches) fell in one hour in Wythe County, VA Monday evening causing flash flooding and road washouts. One of the more impressive stream responses occurred on Reed Creek in Wythe County where the gage at Grahams Forge (GRHV2) crested at 9.14 feet (discharge of 11600 cfs) on the afternoon of the 20th (Flood Stage = 7 feet). This was the 2nd highest stage since continuous records began there in the late 1920s and in terms of flood frequency equates to roughly a 33-year event (Annual Exceedance Probability, AEP of 0.03) per the latest USGS Flood Frequency Report. The highest stage recorded at this gage occurred in April 1977 at 10.01 feet during a period of major flooding in southwest Virginia. Local emergency management official said the flooding was ���historic��� in that area. Over 50 roads were reported to have been closed in Wythe County by flood waters during the event with substantial road damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Little Walker Creek was reported over the road along several portions of Route 601.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet period began early on the 4th as a complex series of disturbances lifted northeast through the Ohio Valley pushing a slow-moving cold front across the area. Rainfall ending 0700 EST on March 4th was confined to the northwestern mountains with amounts to that time from 0.25 to 0.75 inches. Stream gages across parts of the western New and Greenbrier basins were rising rapidly during the overnight and early morning hours of March 4th.  The wet soils and rapid snowmelt were strong contributors to the rapid runoff. Snow water equivalents per the National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center (NOHRSC) in parts of these river basins ranged from around 1��� to as much as 4��� in the higher terrain at the outset of the flooding. The rain and warmer temperatures caused rapid snowmelt the entire day on the 4th along with continued rainfall and there were numerous reports of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were closed due to flooding across the county. A church and several properties were flooded in the Raven area causing significant damage. The Clinch River at Richlands in western Tazewell County crested above the Moderate Flood Stage (12 feet) at 12.29 feet early on the 5th which was the highest stage observed since March, 1998.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A massive Arctic air mass spread across much of the eastern U.S. behind a strong cold front on February 14th.  This front brought high winds, bitterly cold Arctic air, and upslope accumulating snow showers to the mountains of eastern West Virginia, southwest and west central Virginia, and northwest North Carolina.  The combination of the bitterly cold temperatures in the single digits to near -10F at the higher elevations of the mountains combined with northwest winds of 20 to 40 mph produced dangerously low wind chills during the early morning hours of the 15th.  Here is a sample of some of the lowest wind chill readings reported from the southwest and west central Virginia counties within the forecast area:||Bath county - Hot Springs airport observed wind chill readings in the -26F to -31F range from midnight EST to just after noon EST,|Grayson county - several hours of -20F and lower wind chills were observed at the Grayson Highlands State Park,|Smyth county - a wind chill reading of -20F was observed at the Marion-Wytheville Mountain Empire Airport at 815 am EST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station at the Grayson Highlands State Park recorded dangerously low wind chills of -20F or lower during this time frame.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep moist southerly flow aloft continued for an extended period across the Southern Appalachian region March 4th and 5th.  Problems ensued as this warm and humid air mass was undercut by an arctic intrusion throughout the day on March 5th. Lift was enhanced by a wave of low pressure riding northeast from the Deep South across the Southern Atlantic Coastal Plain. Considerable melting in the warm layer lead to a lengthy period of sleet and freezing rain until later in the day when the cold air was deep enough for the precipitation to fall entirely in the form of snow. Ice accumulation was greatest across the Cumberland Plateau and Southwest Virginia. Several inches of snow fell in the higher terrain across Southwest Virginia and Northeast Tennessee following the ice storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inches of snow was reported in Wise.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave with accompanying surface cold front swung through the Mid-Atlantic. Scattered convection formed over the Ohio River Valley, with a favorable atmosphere consisting of SBCAPE values between 1500-2000 J/Kg and increasing deep layered shear over the region allowed for the propagating storms to continue into northern Virginia, with scattered embedded severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported by Cavalier Kennels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of Hurricane Joaquin near the Bahamas and strong high pressure over New England produced strong onshore winds over the Mid-Atlantic. The strength and duration of the onshore winds produced moderate coastal flooding along the Atlantic Coast and Chesapeake Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tidal departure of 3 to 4 feet resulted in moderate flooding along the Chesapeake Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sporadic strong winds in the 40 to 45 mph range late Tuesday, October, 28 and early Wednesday, October, 29 brought down some trees in the Narrows and Pembroke regions of Giles County Virginia. Power outages were reported as trees fell on five spans of power lines near Pembroke late Tuesday evening. Another tree downed a power line that caused an additional power outage in Pembroke and also Narrows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sporadic strong winds in the 40 to 45 mph range late Tuesday, October, 28 and early Wednesday, October, 29 brought down some trees in the Narrows and Pembroke regions of Giles County Virginia. Power outages were reported as trees fell on five spans of power lines near Pembroke late Tuesday evening. Another tree downed a power line that caused an additional power outage in Pembroke and also Narrows. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper low over the Tennessee Valley helped to maintain strong easterly winds into the region, along with bouts of moderate to heavy rainfall totaling one to three inches over the western Piedmont region of Virginia and also in parts of Montgomery and Botetourt Counties. These totals occurred soon after very heavy rain concluded the month of September across a similar location. With a saturated soil, wind gusts of only 30 to 40 mph were enough to bring a large number of trees down across parts this region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and saturated ground allowed for ten trees to be blown down. Trees were blown down in or near the communities of Penhook, Bassett, Henry, Rocky Mount, Union Hall, and Ferrum. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several large rivers including the Dan and lower Roanoke rivers crested in the first few days of the month in response to the heavy rains that fell the last few days of September into October. The synoptic pattern in place at the end of September carried over into early October, featuring an upper level low over the Tennessee Valley and a strong surface ridge over New England combining to maintain a very moist easterly flow into the region.  The forecast rains overspread much of the remainder of the HSA (with the exception of most areas in southeast WV) during the day on the 1st with widespread light moderate amounts. Precipitation totals were in the 0.25��� to 0.75��� range ending at 12Z on the 2nd although a few sites received over an inch including Philpott Dam (PTTV2) 1.20��� and Witts Orchard IFLOWS (WITV2) 1.08���.  The basic pattern was unchanged the following 24 hours with the strong ridge remaining over the northeast and surface low pressure moving slowly along the southeast coast. Bands of moderate to heavy rainfall tracked to the northwest of the surface low into parts of the piedmont but heavier rain was confined mostly south and east of the Blue Ridge divide.  24-hour rain totals ending at 12Z on the 3rd ranged generally from 1 to 3 inches across several southern VA counties, with the top five gage amounts at Maggoddee Creek IFLOWS (MGDV2) 3.19���, King COOP (KIGN7) 3.10���, Eden COOP (EDEN7) 3.06���, Lower Smith IFLOWS (LWSV2) 2.88��� and Dan River at Pine Hall (PNHN7) 2.58���.  Over the ensuing 24-hours the focus of heavy rainfall began to shift generally to the south of the Blacksburg HSA with only moderate rainfall across the North Carolina mountains and adjacent southwest VA. Several areal Flood Warnings were issued during the evening of the 3rd and early on the 4th for counties along and near the Blue Ridge with 1 to 2 inches of rain and several stream gages indicating minor flood conditions.  The top five VA rain gage totals ending 12Z on the 4th included Rocky Mount 3.1 SE CCR (VA-FR-4) 2.29���, Woolwine COOP (WLWV2) 2.28���, Copper Hill 6.2 S CCR (VA-FL-2) 2.21���, Chestnut Knob IFLOWS (CHSV2) 2.04��� and Maggoddee Creek IFLOWS (MGDV2) 1.97. River flood warnings along the Dan River and lower Roanoke River were maintained through this entire period as stages that were starting to fall on the 1st were reversed and crested in the high minor to moderate category on the 4th and 5th of October (see E-3 section below).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least 28 roads were closed county-wide. Dairy View Road and State Line Bridge Road were closed through Monday (October 5th) morning. One road was caved in the 7500 Block of Mt. Cross Road near Tunstle High School Road on Saturday afternoon. There was also significant crop damage across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper low over the Tennessee Valley helped to maintain strong easterly winds into the region, along with bouts of moderate to heavy rainfall totaling one to three inches over the western Piedmont region of Virginia and also in parts of Montgomery and Botetourt Counties. These totals occurred soon after very heavy rain concluded the month of September across a similar location. With a saturated soil, wind gusts of only 30 to 40 mph were enough to bring a large number of trees down across parts this region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and saturated ground allowed for trees to be blown down countywide, twenty-three of which fell, blocking roadways. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of Hurricane Joaquin near the Bahamas and strong high pressure over New England produced strong onshore winds over the Mid-Atlantic. The strength and duration of the onshore winds produced moderate coastal flooding along the Atlantic Coast and Chesapeake Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tidal departure of 2 to 3 feet resulted in moderate flooding along the Rappahannock River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A upper trough continued to deepen across the Great Lakes.  The associated cold front continued to move east through the region.  A warm, moist air mass remained present in advance of the front.  The front was just west of the Blue Ridge at midday.  As the front moved into the warm, unstable air mass, thunderstorms quickly developed near the Blue Ridge, quickly moving eastward into the Piedmont.  A few of these thunderstorms became strong to severe producing damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Campbell County 911 Center reported that a tree was down on Red House Road near its intersection with Rhonda Road, blocking both lanes of the road.  This occurred approximately four miles northeast of Gladys.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms over central Patrick County remained nearly stationary for several hours producing intense rainfall over several small watersheds in the upper Dan River basin. Dual-Pol radar estimated over 4 inches from the storm and Busted Rock #1 IFLOWS gage located near Talbott Reservoir measured a total of 5.57 of which 3.79��� fell in a 3-hour period between 600 and 900 PM EDT (22z-01z). This amount/duration represents somewhere between a 10 to 25-year annual recurrence interval (ARI) per NOAA Atlas 14. Several other gages in the area also reported in excess of 3 inches for the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported 6 to 8 inches of water flowing across Highway 58 where it crosses Big Ivy Creek.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water from Big Cherry Creek flooded a portion of Shotgun Road. The water was estimated to be a least one foot deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet period began early on the 4th as a complex series of disturbances lifted northeast through the Ohio Valley pushing a slow-moving cold front across the area. Rainfall ending 0700 EST on March 4th was confined to the northwestern mountains with amounts to that time from 0.25 to 0.75 inches. Stream gages across parts of the western New and Greenbrier basins were rising rapidly during the overnight and early morning hours of March 4th.  The wet soils and rapid snowmelt were strong contributors to the rapid runoff. Snow water equivalents per the National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center (NOHRSC) in parts of these river basins ranged from around 1��� to as much as 4��� in the higher terrain at the outset of the flooding. The rain and warmer temperatures caused rapid snowmelt the entire day on the 4th along with continued rainfall and there were numerous reports of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding from Wolf Creek affected portions of Route 61 southwest of Narrows. Water was nearing some homes but no evacuations were required. The stream gage on Wolf Creek at Narrows (WOLV2) crested at 10.42 feet at 0615 AM EST (Minor Flood Stage is 10 feet).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper low was located across eastern Canada with a trough extending southward across the Appalachians.  A nearly stationary cold front was draped west to east across the region near the Virginia/North Carolina border.  Moisture values were quite high across the region.  Thus with ample heating and instability, scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed mainly east of the Blue Ridge across the Piedmont and into Southside Virginia.  These thunderstorms produced marginally severe hail and a number of reports of downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Amherst County 911 Center reported that several trees were down along Turkey Mountain Road approximately five miles north of Amherst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper low was located across eastern Canada with a trough extending southward across the Appalachians.  A nearly stationary cold front was draped west to east across the region near the Virginia/North Carolina border.  Moisture values were quite high across the region. These thunderstorms produced marginally severe hail and a number of reports of downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Martinsville Bulletin and News reported that a brief but intense severe thunderstorm toppled trees and knocked out electricity to more than 1700 Appalachian Power customers.  The storm was concentrated in the Bassett area, and in particular in the Riverside Drive, Columbus Drive, and Mary Hunter Drive areas.  Several of these roads were blocked by downed trees and traffic had to be rerouted until the trees were removed from the roadways.  A portion of the roof to the Historic J.D. Bassett Event Center on Riverside Drive was damaged as a result of the storm.  The damage to the roof resulted in significant leaks and allowed water to accumulate four to six inches deep in front of the auditorium stage.  In addition, a very old historic tree on the green in front of the Event Center was also uprooted and destroyed.  There were no injuries as a result of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly approached and moved south through the Mid Atlantic late in the day. Ahead of the front, southerly flow ushered in a warm and moisture air mass, resulting in MLCAPE values between 1000-1500 J/kg with marginal shear. Numerous showers and thunderstorms with embedded severe formed to the southwest and progressed to the northeast prior to the cold frontal passage.

Scattered severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening as a strong upper level disturbance passed across the central Appalachians. Thunderstorm activity was especially enhanced due to strong surface convergence east of the Blue Ridge Mountains as high pressure in the low levels wedged southward from New England, providing easterly wind flow across the Piedmont, with southwesterly winds observed around 3000 feet above the surface.  Upper level ridging across the southeast and mid-Atlantic states supported strong surface heating, which allowed CAPE (Convective Available Potential Energy) values to exceed 2000J/Kg across much of the region.  Mid-level winds across the northern half of the forecast area increased to around 40 knots during the afternoon, aiding in storm organization, while deep tropical moisture across the region pooling ahead of the front aided in producing strong wind gusts due to increased precipitation loading in the downdrafts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to nickel-size hail was observed at Staunton River High School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong cold front passed across the central Appalachians during the evening of June 27th.  Storm intensity increased east of a lee surface trough across the Piedmont of North Carolinas into Virginia.  Heavy precipitation loading occurred with some of the storms due to a very moist atmosphere, where precipitable water values were observed exceeding 1.5 inches.  Damaging wind gusts also occurred due to downward mixing of momentum associated with the 30 to 40 knot mean southwesterly lower and mid tropospheric wind flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of water was observed along the 300 block of John Redd Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms associated with a trough of low pressure produced damaging winds across portions of central, south central, and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were uprooted. Three houses were damaged. One vehicle was crushed. An apparent microburst caused the damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm and moist air mass in advance of a strong late spring cold front brought the first significant severe thunderstorm event to the Blacksburg, VA National Weather Service forecast area of the 2015 calendar year.  The bulk of the severe thunderstorms were mainly east of the Blue Ridge, but began around 530 pm EDT in the Southern Shenandoah Valley and northern Blue Ridge counties within the forecast area.  Another round of severe thunderstorms began around 1000 pm EDT near Lynchburg and moved east across the Piedmont, affecting some of the same counties impacted by storms earlier in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public provided a report via Facebook of nickel-sized hail in the Pedlar Mills area or about three miles northeast of Naola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong cold front passed across the central Appalachians during the evening of June 27th.  Storm intensity increased east of a lee surface trough across the Piedmont of North Carolinas into Virginia.  Heavy precipitation loading occurred with some of the storms due to a very moist atmosphere, where precipitable water values were observed exceeding 1.5 inches.  Damaging wind gusts also occurred due to downward mixing of momentum associated with the 30 to 40 knot mean southwesterly lower and mid tropospheric wind flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in the vicinity of Martinsville.  One tree feel on a parked vehicle on Spring Road, while other fallen trees took down power lines on Perdue Street and Spruce Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level disturbances passed across the central Appalachians during the afternoon and evening, triggering strong to severe thunderstorms across the forecast area.  The disturbance that passed across the area during the afternoon interacted with a lee-side trough to bring strong thunderstorms to the Piedmont.  A second, stronger upper level disturbance arrived around sunset, passing east along the Highway 460 corridor, resulting in locally strong winds and heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down along Route 704.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong moisture advection over the Mid-Atlantic led to dew points into the upper 70s and isolated spots reporting 80. Showers and thunderstorms formed ahead of an advancing cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a road closed due to being washed out. There was a five foot drop off in the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening of June 18th as subtle upper level disturbances passed across the central Appalachians.  Thirty to forty knot winds in the mid levels of the atmosphere provided some difluence aloft, while strong surface heating due to upper level ridging across the southeast and mid-Atlantic states provided strong surface-based instability.  Deep tropical moisture across the region provided precipitation loading to the storms, which added to the increased thunderstorm wind speeds.
Scattered severe thunderstorms in advance of low pressure and a weak cold front produced damaging winds, large hail, and one weak tornado across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed around Stony Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front passed through the area while an upper-level trough approached from the west. A southerly flow behind the front caused an unstable atmosphere, and moderate amounts of instability developed due to colder air aloft ahead of the approaching trough. There was enough instability for some thunderstorms to become severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were down in the Warrenton area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in advance of low pressure and a weak cold front produced damaging winds, large hail, and one weak tornado across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were downed in the Fine Creeks Mills area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong moisture advection over the Mid-Atlantic led to dew points into the upper 70s and isolated spots reporting 80. Showers and thunderstorms formed ahead of an advancing cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Whiting Road was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms associated with a larger cluster of storms advanced southeast through the region. A large number of trees were downed across the area, and some storms produced large hail during the late afternoon and early evening. An isolated severe storm not associated with the thunderstorm complex generated quarter size hail much earlier in the day in Bedford.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew a tree down along Saxkey Road. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bowing line of severe convection in association with an outflow boundary moved southeast across Southwest Virginia and Northeast Tennessee during the late afternoon through early evening hours. Atmospheric shear was more than adequate along with moderate to high instability. Widespread wind damage occurred during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms associated with a trough of low pressure produced damaging winds and three weak tornadoes across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Falling tree downed a transformer and resulted in a small brush fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms associated with a trough of low pressure produced damaging winds and three weak tornadoes across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Boats were capsized and a roof was blown off a building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Destabilization occurred west of the Blue Ridge with MLCAPE 1500-2000 J/Kg forming by the afternoon hours. Backing of winds also led to increased shear profiles, which combined with the increased instability produced isolated thunderstorms ahead of an approaching cold front and a line of showers and thunderstorms right along the line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down across Highway 11 about 1/2 mile south of Edinburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly approached and moved south through the Mid Atlantic late in the day. Ahead of the front, southerly flow ushered in a warm and moisture air mass, resulting in MLCAPE values between 1000-1500 J/kg with marginal shear. Numerous showers and thunderstorms with embedded severe formed to the southwest and progressed to the northeast prior to the cold frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn roof was blown off and several large tree limbs were snapped along Earl Sine Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level ridging across the southeast and mid-Atlantic states resulting in strong daytime heating, which drove surface-based CAPE into the 2000 to 2500 J/Kg range across portions of the forecast area.  A few storms developed during early afternoon, one of which intensified to severe levels despite the lack of any significant upper level support.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was blown down near the intersection of Highway 460 and Irving Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in advance of a cold front produced damaging winds across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were downed around Wilsons.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of Tropical Storm Bill moved into the Mid-Atlantic causing moisture to increase. Showers and thunderstorms led to heavy rain across the Interstate 95 corridor and flash flooding occurred. Some rivers also went to flood into June 21, 2015.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 1 was closed due to high water over Neabsco Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Destabilization occurred west of the Blue Ridge with MLCAPE 1500-2000 J/Kg forming by the afternoon hours. Backing of winds also led to increased shear profiles, which combined with the increased instability produced isolated thunderstorms ahead of an approaching cold front and a line of showers and thunderstorms right along the line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported blocking St Luke Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary acted as the triggering point for an initial line of showers and thunderstorms, with many of the storms becoming severe with strong moisture advection combining with diurnal heating and leading to an unstable air mass. The remnants of Tropical System Bill moved through the Mid-Atlantic overnight, continuing the severe threat late into the early part of the overnight hours. As winds backed with the tropical system, increasing directional and magnitude shear led to the development of tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported at Knyg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong cold front passed across the central Appalachians during the evening of June 27th.  Storm intensity increased east of a lee surface trough across the Piedmont of North Carolinas into Virginia.  Heavy precipitation loading occurred with some of the storms due to a very moist atmosphere, where precipitable water values were observed exceeding 1.5 inches.  Damaging wind gusts also occurred due to downward mixing of momentum associated with the 30 to 40 knot mean southwesterly lower and mid tropospheric wind flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a mobile home, causing a hot water heater to catch fire that resulted in minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall developed across portions of the southern Appalachian mountains as clearing ahead of a slow-moving surface low over the Cumberland Gap allowed instabilities to rise into the 2000 J/Kg range along with good upper divergence. Developing storms and cell mergers produced rainfall rates of 1.5���/hr with totals up to 3-4��� in isolated storms. A Flash Flood Warning was issued for Tazewell County at 737 PM EDT where radar had showed 1 to 2 inches of rain had fallen with more expected. Total rainfall amounts reached 2.5 to 3 inches in a 3-hour period ending around 02z (10 PM on the 5th) over parts of northeastern Tazewell County which produced substantial flash flooding and debris flows in several locations. The worst flooding occurred along Laurel Fork near the town of Pocahontas where 25 homes, 5 businesses and 2 mobile homes were damaged or destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Laurel Creek flooded about half of the town of Pocahontas. 25 homes, 5 businesses and 2 mobile homes were damaged or destroyed. Total damage estimates reached $3.5 million, primarily due to a single business that was uninsured and destroyed. Multiple roads across northeast Tazewell County were reported to be closed due to flooding and mudslides as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure was wedged against the eastern face of the Appalachians, while a deep low pressure system passed across the central Plains states.  The tight pressure gradient between the two features resulted in 60 to 65 knot southeasterly low level jet  along the central Appalachians.  Winds were particularly strong on the west side of the actual ridge lines, where downslope flow acted to draw the winds of the low level jet toward the surface, resulting in surface gusts 50 to nearly 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southeasterly winds resulted in isolated reports of downed trees in Bland County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving cold front pushed eastward into the Appalachians/mid-Atlantic region November 18-19  bringing widespread moderate to heavy rainfall. Generally 1 to 2.5 inches fell across much of southwest Virginia in the 24 hours ending at 7 AM EST (12z). Several reports of small streams flooding were received and the Roanoke River at Salem (SPSV2) crested just above Minor flood stage closing the Mill Lane bridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Plum Creek was reported out of its banks and covering Grant Drive just east of Radford near the intersection of Route 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Retreating high pressure to the north ahead of an approaching warm front let to a CAD situation, with temperatures hovering right around freezing for a good portion of the day for very isolated spots at the highest elevations of the Blue Ridge. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trace of ice was reported falling from the trees at Wintergreen Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very wet pattern developed in the days leading up to and through Christmas beginning around the 21st.  A deep southerly flow between a trough over the western U.S. and powerhouse ridge off the east coast brought extremely high moisture and warmth across much of the eastern U.S. during this period. Precipitable water readings were 2 to 3 times normal late December values for several days and numerous record high temperatures occurred across the entire region. Rainfall was generally light to moderate on the 21st and 22nd with 0.25��� up to around 1.00��� each day. Heavier rains developed on the 23rd as low pressure tracked out of the Gulf coast toward the southern Appalachians. Rainfall of 1��� to 2.5��� ending at 12z (7 AM EST) on the 24th prompted the first of several river Flood warnings along the lower Dan and Roanoke river basins.  Heavy rains continued into the 24th with several bands of more intense rainfall across mountains and then across the Piedmont. A swath of 1 to 2+ inches of rain fell across portions of Pittsylvania, Halifax and Charlotte counties in a 6-hour period ending at 18z (1 PM EST) on the 24th producing flash flooding. Numerous roads were reported closed by VA Department of Transportation by mid-afternoon on the 24th across all three of these counties. River flooding developed along the Dan and lower Roanoke rivers as a result of the runoff with several gauges reaching moderate flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least six or seven roads were closed across Halifax County from Paces up through Nathalie.

Several roads on the Halifax County side of the river were closed including Black Walnut Road with several feet of water over the road. This was the highest crest on the Randolph gage since January 27, 2010 (26.86 ft.).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms formed along a nearly stationary frontal boundary draped across the Mid-Atlantic. Increased dew points on the southern side of the front ranged from the mid 60s to lower 70s. These dew points along with temps in the upper 80s resulted in instability between 1500 and 2000 J/kg. The higher instability combined with deep layer shear values 30-40 knots led to the development of afternoon and evening scattered severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three large trees were reported down at the residence in the 10000 block of Milstead Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through the Mid-Atlantic region. Well above normal temperatures occurred across the region while moisture streamed in from the south. Heavy rain led to localized flash flooding across the Baltimore and Washington DC metros.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gauge at Huntington Park at Cameron Run reached a flood stage of 5.5 feet. It peaked at 5.97 feet at 18:20 EST. Water began to cover undeveloped land within Huntington Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front made its way southward across the Piedmont during the evening, supported aloft by an upper level disturbance passing across the central Appalachians in the otherwise upper level ridging environment covering much of the southeast and mid-Atlantic states.  The cold front and disturbance provided increased instability, allowing strong to severe thunderstorms to develop in the very warm moist air south of the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down on Salem Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the most high-impact flood/flash flooding episodes in recent years in the Blacksburg Hydrologic Service Area (HSA) developed over a several day period but culminated September 29th. An unusual weather pattern became established around September 25th with very slow-moving and strong high pressure building across New England and low pressure over the southeastern U.S. combining to bring strong easterly flow into the southern Mid-Atlantic along with abundant moisture. Rainfall ending 12z on the 25th was generally confined to the southern portions of the HSA with up to 1 inch in parts of the North Carolina piedmont into southside Virginia. The rains became much heavier and widespread over the next two days with 1 to 4 inches ending at 12z on the 26th and another 1 to nearly 5 inches ending at 12z on the 27th. Over the next five days rainfall was nearly constant in some areas with pulses of heavier rainfall but with the heaviest amounts concentrating across the VA Blue Ridge mountains, mainly in Patrick, Floyd, Roanoke, Henry and Franklin counties. The maximum 5-day rainfall totals ending at 12Z (8 AM) on the 30th ranged from 10 to 16 inches in parts of Patrick and Floyd counties. Some of the higher totals equate to anywhere from a 100 to 500-year event per NOAA Atlas 14. The extremely wet soils set the stage for the severe flash flooding early on September 29th into the afternoon. Several bands of intense rainfall tracked across parts of the same counties mentioned above and also including Montgomery County over a 6 to 12 hour period from 0200 LST to 1400 lST (06z-18z) prompting the issuance of two Flash Flood Warnings for parts of eight counties and several cities at 943 AM (EDT) and 1019 (EDT) AM.  It was difficult to differentiate between the onset of flash flooding from the longer duration flooding that was developing or underway as well. No matter the type, by mid-morning numerous reports of flooding and several high water rescues had been reported across a wide area, but most notably in parts of Patrick, Floyd, Montgomery, Roanoke and Henry counties.  Local states of emergency were declared in Floyd and Patrick counties due to the widespread flood impacts with water rescues, some homes damaged and dozens of roads closed due to flooding or trees brought down in the saturated soil.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported across Route 11 in Radford. The automated rain gage at the Radford River gage (RDFV2) measured 2.42 in one hour ending 0600 LST, which is very close to the 100-year recurrence interval (0.01 annual exceedance probability) for this location and duration.

No matter the type, by mid-morning numerous reports of flooding and several high water rescues had been reported across a wide area, but most notably in parts of Patrick, Floyd, Montgomery, Roanoke and Henry counties.  Local states of emergency were declared in Floyd and Patrick counties due to the widespread flood impacts with water rescues, some homes damaged and dozens of roads closed due to flooding or trees brought down in the saturated soil.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding occurred across the county but was concentrated in Route 8 corridor. Several  swift water rescues were made and numerous roads were closed and many damaged. A van was swept off of Meadows Creek Road and the driver, a college student, was rescued. In addition, 13 homes were destroyed, 12 homes received major damage, 15 homes minor damage, and 42 other homes affected, for a total loss of $2.3 million dollars. The worst flooding appeared to be in the Sowers Mill Dam Road and Little River Road areas. According to county officials additional commercial and agricultural losses ran about $400,000. Road damage totaled an additional $400,000 per the Virginia Department of Transportation. Local officials described it as one of the worst weather disasters in county history. The Little River which runs through much of Floyd County was responsible for much of the damage. Residents in the Sowers Mill Dam Road area said the river rose 20 to 25 feet during the flood. The river gage on the Little River further downstream at Graysontown along the Pulaski-Montgomery county line reached a stage of 12.68 feet (flood stage = 8 ft), the 3rd highest at the current datum and highest since Hurricane Agnes in 1972.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported along Morningside Drive west of Sanville. Blackberry Creek IFLOWS gage (BBBV2) rose from around 2 feet at 5AM to crest at 8.2 feet (Minor Flood Stage = 6 ft) by early early afternoon. Some crawl spaces were flooded along Blackberry Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front made its way southward across the Piedmont during the evening, supported aloft by an upper level disturbance passing across the central Appalachians in the otherwise upper level ridging environment covering much of the southeast and mid-Atlantic states.  The cold front and disturbance provided increased instability, allowing strong to severe thunderstorms to develop in the very warm moist air south of the cold front.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roanoke River flooded through the city of Roanoke due mainly to the very heavy rains across the upper basin. Rainfall in the city was generally around 1 inch during the day but up to 4 inches fell across the headwaters in Montgomery County. The USGS gage and NWS forecast point at Walnut Street (RONV2) rose very rapidly, reaching flood stage (10 ft) by early afternoon and cresting at 14.40 ft in the Moderate Flood Category and  the 10th highest stage on record (data at the current location since 1937). The recurrence interval per USGS data on the flooding at the Roanoke gage indicated approximately a 10-year event (0.1 annual exceedance probability). Several roads close to the Roanoke River were closed but no substantial damage was reported. Water rescues from three vehicles on Bennington Street were  performed during the evening of the 29th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding turned into river flooding across the eastern two-thirds of Montgomery County as runoff across the upper Roanoke River basin from the extremely heavy rainfall continued. Mean areal precipitation was a solid 2 to 4 inches across the South Fork Roanoke River basin and the gage at Shawsville (SHAV2) crested above the Major Flood Category (9 ft) at 9.32 feet (Flood Stage = 5 feet) the 2nd highest on record (since 1960) and highest since 1979 (9.27 ft). Precipitation was somewhat lower in the North Fork Roanoke River basin, generally 1 to 3 inches and the gage near Ironto (IROV2) crested at 10.1 feet (Flood Stage = 9 ft). On the mainstem Roanoke River at Lafayette (LAAV2) just below the confluence of the North and South Forks the crest reached 12.16 ft. in the Moderate Flood category and the 6th highest on record since 1944 and highest since the remains of Hurricane Jeanne in September, 2004. In addition numerous roads remained closed across the county into the late afternoon and evening due to slow to recede flood waters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 1 to 2 inches by 8 AM (EST) across western Franklin County continued through early afternoon with an additional 1.5 to 3 inches in the far western sections. These included Runnet Bag Road, Coles Creek Road, Madcap Road, Ruritan Road, Wades Gap Road, Boones Mill Road, Five Mile Mountain Road, Dillons Mill/Western Comer Road. The IFLOWS stream gage on Maggodee Creek (MGDV2) reached Action Stage of 5 feet by 1215 PM (EST) and crested at 1245 PM at 6.2 feet, just below Flood Stage of 7 feet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding and flash flooding was widespread across Patrick County. Numerous creeks and rivers were reported out of banks with dozens of road closures across the county for much of the day. An apartment complex was evacuated due to a mudslide and multiple swift water rescues were carried out. Polebridge Creek west of Sanville was reported out of its banks and Big Ivy Creek flooded a portion of Route 58 southeast of Meadows of Dan. The Smith River gage at Woolwine (SMWV2) reached its highest crest on record (data only back to 1995) at 16.11 feet (6300 cfs) at 500 AM (EST) on the 29th (old record 12.20 ft. on 9/28/2004). This is a revised value based on a post-flood analysis by VA Department of Environmental Quality. The historic (1921) Bob White covered bridge over the Smith River near Woolwine was washed away as the river rose. Total damages in Patrick County were estimated at $4 million about half from road and infrastructure damage and half from agricultural losses.

Other road closures that continued into the evening included the low water bridge over the Roanoke River at Mill Lane, Texas Street at Carter Way,  East Riverside at Front Avenue, Eddy Street, Colorado Street, Apperson Drive, Mill Lane at Mountain Avenue, West Salem Mobile Home Park and West Riverside Mobile Home Village.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were closed throughout the day across parts of Roanoke County as 1 to 3 inches of rain, with isolated 4 inch amounts from early on the 29th through midday. A water rescue from a car was needed along Bent Mountain Road. The Roanoke River at Glenvar gage (GNVV2) rose to 16.53 feet during the afternoon of the 29th due to the very heavy rain across the upper Roanoke River basin. This stage was above the defined Major Flood Category (16 ft) and the 4th highest crest since records began in 1992 on this gage. It was slightly below a 10-year recurrence interval (0.1 annual exceedance probability). Several roads near the river were closed due to the flood waters. Mason Creek (MSCV2) north of Salem in the county crested at 7.5 ft (Flood Stage = 7 ft)  at 1400 LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Instability associated with an upper level disturbance combined with strong daytime heating to trigger spotty shower and thunderstorm development along the Interstate 64 corridor.  Most of the storms were weak, however one storm intensified to severe intensity for a brief period of time in Rockbridge County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large limbs were blown down by thunderstorm winds.

An unusual weather pattern became established around September 25th with very slow-moving and strong high pressure building across New England and low pressure over the southeastern U.S. combining to bring strong easterly flow into the southern Mid-Atlantic along with abundant moisture. Rainfall ending 12z on the 25th was generally confined to the southern portions of the HSA with up to 1 inch in parts of the North Carolina piedmont into southside Virginia. The rains became much heavier and widespread over the next two days with 1 to 4 inches ending at 12z on the 26th and another 1 to nearly 5 inches ending at 12z on the 27th. Over the next five days rainfall was nearly constant in some areas with pulses of heavier rainfall but with the heaviest amounts concentrating across the VA Blue Ridge mountains, mainly in Patrick, Floyd, Roanoke, Henry and Franklin counties. The maximum 5-day rainfall totals ending at 12Z (8 AM) on the 30th ranged from 10 to 16 inches in parts of Patrick and Floyd counties. Some of the higher totals equate to anywhere from a 100 to 500-year event per NOAA Atlas 14. The extremely wet soils set the stage for the severe flash flooding early on September 29th into the afternoon. Several bands of intense rainfall tracked across parts of the same counties mentioned above and also including Montgomery County over a 6 to 12 hour period from 0200 LST to 1400 lST (06z-18z) prompting the issuance of two Flash Flood Warnings for parts of eight counties and several cities at 943 AM (EDT) and 1019 (EDT) AM.  It was difficult to differentiate between the onset of flash flooding from the longer duration flooding that was developing or underway as well. No matter the type, by mid-morning numerous reports of flooding and several high water rescues had been reported across a wide area, but most notably in parts of Patrick, Floyd, Montgomery, Roanoke and Henry counties.  Local states of emergency were declared in Floyd and Patrick counties due to the widespread flood impacts with water rescues, some homes damaged and dozens of roads closed due to flooding or trees brought down in the saturated soil.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of roads flooded and some badly damaged across Montgomery County. The biggest impacts were in the western half of the county where 3 to 5 inches of rain fell in a 6-hour period between 0700 LST and 1300 LST. The asphalt on Laurel Mill Ridge road was reportedly washed away. Meadow Creek Road had at least one foot of water rushing over it. Duck Pond Road was closed from Oak Lane to Smithfield. Radford Road was closed, Route 8 in Riner was partially flooded and an earth retaining wall gave way. Toms Creek was flooding a portion of Mt. Zion Road near Brooksfield Road. Plantation was closed due to 6 to 12 inches of water over the road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 4 to 7 inches across the area caused several roads to be closed due to flooding in eastern Pulaski County. Route 605 was closed in several locations along with portions of Routes 665, 613 and 626. The river gage on the Little River further  at Graysontown (GRYV2) along the Pulaski-Montgomery county line reached a stage of 12.68 feet (flood stage = 8 ft), the 3rd highest at the current datum and highest since Hurricane Agnes in 1972. The discharge of 23100 cubic feet per second equated to approximately a 50-year recurrence interval per USGS information.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Only isolated and non-severe thunderstorm activity was observed, developing with afternoon heating.  However, one of the storms put down cloud-to-ground lightning which caused a fire at a business.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a HVAC business just northeast of the community of Madison Heights.  Two 16 x 32 foot garage buildings housing the HVAC business were damaged, one of which burned to the ground and was completely destroyed.  The other was partially destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front made its way southward across the Piedmont during the evening, supported aloft by an upper level disturbance passing across the central Appalachians in the otherwise upper level ridging environment covering much of the southeast and mid-Atlantic states.  The cold front and disturbance provided increased instability, allowing strong to severe thunderstorms to develop in the very warm moist air south of the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-size hail fell from Mud Lick Road through Grandin Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: LUA reported visibilities of one quarter mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lee trough formed out ahead of an approaching cold front, acting as a surface trigger for scattered convection. Lack of shear led to the storms being pulse-like in nature, based upon increased instability values.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage was reported at the Lockn Music Festival. Report included damage to the stage and campgrounds. The portable bathrooms were knocked over, with multiple trees reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain event as low pressure moved through the Mid-Atlantic ahead of a slow moving cold front. Southerly flow ahead of the low pressure tapped into a moisture rich atmosphere over the Gulf of Mexico. While thunderstorms were isolated due to weaker shear and instability, the heaviest of the precipitation was able to bring down gusty winds from aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were reported down, with two HVAC until dislodged in an area of East Market St and Catoctin Circle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Combination of surface trough and passing shortwave aloft provided the forcing to trigger scattered convection. Shear was minimal but MUCAPE values were analyzed to be around 2000 J/kg at the time of initiation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tree limbs were down in the road near Graves Mountain Lodge.

Heavy rain event as low pressure moved through the Mid-Atlantic ahead of a slow moving cold front. Southerly flow ahead of the low pressure tapped into a moisture rich atmosphere over the Gulf of Mexico. While thunderstorms were isolated due to weaker shear and instability, the heaviest of the precipitation was able to bring down gusty winds from aloft. This also led to flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gauge on Limestone Branch near Leesburg reached a flood stage of 7 feet. It peaked at 9.13 feet at 22:30 EST. Greenfield Farm Lane and another access road began to flood and field flooding near the stream became widespread. A home near route 15 along the stream began to flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across the Mid-Atlantic. Showers formed in a very moist atmosphere across the region. Heavy rainfall produced flooding across the Interstate 95 corridor in the afternoon and heavy rain across Central Virginia in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several vehicles were stranded in high water under a railroad overpass on Old Ivy Road.
